title: Pelican to Murder — Level 1 Training Detective
description: Level 1 version with guided clues, direct evidence, and first-time player support.

answerKey.method: Served Lena the unmarked praline cocktail after moving the allergy marker
answerKey.motive: To keep Lena from replacing her as lead event bartender
answerKey.keyEvidence: Allergy marker text, service rail photo, and register camera still
answerKey.explanation: Tessa knew the blue-pick allergy system and was the only person confirmed at the service rail when Lena’s marked glass became unmarked. Her text made the marker important, the photo proved the marker moved, and the register camera placed her at the swap.

publicClues.title: Case Opened
publicClues.text: Pelican to Murder begins as a live investigation. This session is Level 1: Training Detective Case.

publicClues.title: Difficulty Loaded
publicClues.text: This is Level 1: Training Detective Case. Evidence and answers are unique to this level.

publicClues.title: Checkpoint 1 Available
publicClues.text: Round 1 is ready. Answer the checkpoint after reviewing the drink marker.

publicClues.title: Checkpoint 2 Available
publicClues.text: Round 2 is ready. Compare timeline movement before answering.

publicClues.title: Checkpoint 3 Available
publicClues.text: Round 3 is ready. Digital evidence now matters.

publicClues.title: Checkpoint 4 Available
publicClues.text: Round 4 is ready. Find the motive evidence.

publicClues.title: Final Accusation Open
publicClues.text: Final questions are open. Complete all 10 total questions before the lock.

phone.title: Phone Evidence 1
phone.text: Lena saved Tessa as an emergency allergy contact because Tessa usually handled her drinks.

phone.title: Phone Evidence 2
phone.text: A nine-second call from Tessa came in just before Lena returned to the host table.

messages.title: Messages Evidence 1
messages.text: Tessa texted: “I marked yours blue like always.”

messages.title: Messages Evidence 2
messages.text: Lena replied that the venue owner wanted a new lead bartender if the launch went well.

maps.title: Maps Evidence 1
maps.text: Lena’s phone stayed near the host table until the drink pickup.

maps.title: Maps Evidence 2
maps.text: Tessa’s staff tracker stayed behind the service rail during the drink swap window.

bank.title: Bank Evidence 1
bank.text: Tessa had a pending payroll dispute with the venue.

bank.title: Bank Evidence 2
bank.text: A promotion bonus would only go to the bartender assigned to future mystery nights.

photos.title: Photos Evidence 1
photos.text: A guest photo shows Lena’s first glass with a blue allergy pick.

photos.title: Photos Evidence 2
photos.text: A later photo shows the glass in Lena’s hand without the blue pick.

social.title: Social Evidence 1
social.text: A short guest clip catches Tessa pulling two glasses together on the rail.

social.title: Social Evidence 2
social.text: The clip caption says the bar was “crazy behind the scenes” before the toast.

contacts.title: Contacts Evidence 1
contacts.text: Tessa is listed in Lena’s phone under “allergy safe drinks.”

contacts.title: Contacts Evidence 2
contacts.text: The venue owner is saved under “new bartender talk.”

notes.title: Notes Evidence 1
notes.text: Detective note: the blue pick is not decoration; it marks Lena’s safe drink.

notes.title: Notes Evidence 2
notes.text: Detective note: a suspect must know the allergy system to make the death look accidental.

files.title: Files Evidence 1
files.text: Register camera still shows Tessa alone at the service rail for 19 seconds.

files.title: Files Evidence 2
files.text: Staff schedule shows Tessa was the only bartender assigned to Lena’s drink station.

browser.title: Browser Evidence 1
browser.text: Tessa searched the staff handbook section on termination rules.

browser.title: Browser Evidence 2
browser.text: The same device searched “can a bar be liable for wrong allergy drink.”

rounds.objective: Answer the final five questions to close the case.
rounds.dialogue: The final accusation window is open. Choose the culprit, method, motive, key evidence, and the statement that fails.
rounds.countdownReview: The final window is closing. Submit all answers before the case locks.

openingNarration: Detectives, welcome to Pelican to Murder. You are playing Level 1: Training Detective Case. Study each app carefully. Every level has its own truth path, evidence trail, and final answer set.
revealScript: Case closed. This was Level 1: Training Detective Case. The culprit was Tessa Marchand. The method: Served Lena the unmarked praline cocktail after moving the allergy marker. The motive: To keep Lena from replacing her as lead event bartender. The proof: Allergy marker text, service rail photo, and register camera still.

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 1: Which object makes the scene suspicious?
Checkpoint 2: Who is first confirmed at the service rail?
Checkpoint 3: Which photo clue matters most?

options.label: A receipt with an unusually large tip
Two photos showing the blue marker disappear
A wet bar mat under the rail

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 4: What gives Tessa a motive?

options.label: A dispute over the karaoke playlist
Fear of losing the lead bartender role
A fake vendor bill from the office
A late food ticket from the kitchen

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 5: Which clue locks in the swap?

options.label: The patio map near the exit
The register camera still of Tessa at the glass

questions.prompt: Final 2: How was the murder carried out?

options.label: Tessa served the unmarked praline cocktail
A shove near the patio gate
A broken glass cut during the toast
To win a cocktail contest prize

questions.prompt: Final 4: Which evidence best proves it?

options.label: Blue marker photos plus register camera
Cole said he stayed at sound
Owen said he was in the office
Brielle said she filmed the toast
Tessa claimed she never touched the second glass

briefingSegments.title: Level 1 Briefing: A Simple Night Turns Suspicious
briefingSegments.text: Pelican to Mars is the kind of Baton Rouge bar where regulars know the bartenders, the bartenders know every order, and private events still feel personal. Tonight was supposed to be an easy launch party: music, photos, sponsor drinks, and a small crowd celebrating a new weekly nightlife series. Lena Broussard arrived early because she liked knowing how things worked. She checked the bar setup, watched the service rail, and asked questions about the drink markers used for allergies. For a Training Detective case, start with the obvious: who touched the drink, who knew the system, and who had a reason to make a simple mistake look accidental.

briefingSegments.title: The Crime: A Drink That Should Have Been Safe
briefingSegments.text: Lena collapsed shortly after receiving what should have been her allergy-safe cocktail. At first, the room treated it like a sudden medical emergency. Then the staff noticed the warning marker was not where it should have been. The glass that reached Lena did not match the protection system the bar claimed to use. This case is built to teach the basics of detective work: follow the object, track who handled it, and do not assume a crowded room means the truth is complicated. Somewhere between the service rail, the register camera, and the final handoff, the safe drink became dangerous.

briefingSegments.title: The Victim: Lena Broussard
briefingSegments.text: Lena was not a random guest. She had helped organize small nightlife events before, and people trusted her because she remembered details other people ignored. She knew the staff schedule, the bar setup, and the difference between a harmless mix-up and a warning sign. Recently, Lena had been considered for a larger role managing future event service, which made some people nervous. She was direct, observant, and difficult to fool. If someone wanted to stop her, they did not need an elaborate plan. They only needed access, timing, and the confidence that a busy bar would call it an accident.

briefingSegments.title: The Circle: Familiar Faces, Simple Motives
briefingSegments.text: Tessa Marchand worked behind the bar and knew the drink marker system better than anyone. Owen Landry handled sponsor details and wanted the event to look profitable. Brielle Hart was focused on photos and public attention. Cole Vinet managed music and timing. Marisol Vega moved between staff areas like she belonged everywhere. In this level, every suspect has a reason to be nearby, but not every reason is strong enough to explain the crime. Your job is to separate presence from opportunity, stress from motive, and rumor from proof.

briefingSegments.title: Your Assignment: Learn the Pattern
briefingSegments.text: This is a Training Detective case. Do not chase every shadow. Start with the cleanest path: the victim, the drink, the marker, the people with access, and the evidence that confirms movement. The app icons will unlock evidence over time. Read the Phone and Messages clues carefully. Check photos and service records when they appear. When checkpoint questions open, answer before the next round starts. Every correct answer builds your final detective score. The case begins when the briefing ends. Watch the bar, follow the glass, and prove what really happened.
